title: Cellulitis & Skin Abscess
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Primary Care, Infectious Disease
objectives: Classify skin and soft tissue infections as purulent vs non-purulent
Stratify severity (mild, moderate, severe) per IDSA guidelines
Understand when MRSA coverage is indicated
Apply appropriate antibiotic selection based on classification
Recognize necrotizing soft tissue infection requiring emergent surgery

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA SSTI Guidelines 2014
keyPoints: Purulent infections: S. aureus including MRSA
I&D is primary treatment for abscesses

vignette.text:
A 42-year-old man presents with 3 days of progressively worsening left lower leg redness, swelling, warmth, and pain. He reports a small cut on his shin while gardening 5 days ago. No drainage or fluctuance. No history of MRSA. No IV drug use.

Past Medical History: Type 2 DM (A1c 8.2%), Obesity (BMI 33), Tinea pedis (chronic)

Medications: Metformin, Atorvastatin

Allergies: NKDA

skin.affected_area: Left lower leg - 15 x 8 cm area of erythema with distinct margins extending from mid-shin to ankle
skin.characteristics: Warmth, edema, tenderness to palpation, NO fluctuance, NO crepitus, NO bullae, NO purulent drainage
skin.other: Interdigital maceration and scaling between toes bilaterally (tinea pedis)

physicalExam.vascular: Palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally

questions.stem: This patient has no drainage or fluctuance.
questions.question: How are skin and soft tissue infections classified per IDSA?

options.text: All cellulitis requires MRSA coverage
All skin infections are treated the same
Drainage presence doesn't affect management

IDSA Classification: NON-PURULENT: Cellulitis/erysipelas WITHOUT abscess, drainage, or exudate; Primary pathogen = Group A Strep (S. pyogenes) and other beta-hemolytic strep; PURULENT: Abscess, carbuncle, furuncle, OR cellulitis WITH purulent drainage; Primary pathogen = S. aureus (including MRSA); SEVERITY (both types): MILD: No systemic signs (afebrile, normal vitals); MODERATE: Systemic signs present (fever, tachycardia) but not SIRS; SEVERE: SIRS, failed oral therapy, immunocompromised, or signs of deeper infection (bullae, crepitus); This patient: NON-PURULENT + MODERATE (fever)

questions.explanation: IDSA SSTI Classification: CLASSIFICATION BY APPEARANCE: NON-PURULENT: Cellulitis: Spreading skin infection involving dermis and subcutaneous tissue. Erysipelas: Superficial cellulitis with sharp, raised borders (classically face/legs). NO abscess, NO purulent drainage, NO exudate. Primary pathogens: Beta-hemolytic streptococci (Group A most common), Occasionally MSSA. PURULENT: Abscess (fluctuant collection), Carbuncle, Furuncle/boil, Cellulitis WITH purulent drainage or exudate. Primary pathogen: S. aureus - including CA-MRSA (up to 59% in some studies). SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION: MILD: No systemic symptoms. MODERATE: Systemic symptoms present (fever, malaise) but not meeting SIRS criteria. SEVERE: SIRS criteria (T>38°C or <36°C, HR>90, RR>24, WBC>12K or <4K), Immunocompromised, Failed oral antibiotics, Signs of deeper infection (bullae, skin sloughing, crepitus, hypotension). This patient: Non-purulent (no drainage) + Moderate severity (fever 100.6°F, WBC 12.4K, but not meeting full SIRS).

sources.text: StatPearls: Cellulitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has non-purulent cellulitis without MRSA risk factors.
questions.question: What is the treatment approach for non-purulent cellulitis?

options.text: IV antibiotics required for all cases
All non-purulent cellulitis needs MRSA coverage
Cultures required before starting antibiotics

NON-PURULENT CELLULITIS TREATMENT: PRIMARY TARGET = STREPTOCOCCI (always penicillin-sensitive); MILD (no systemic signs): Oral: Penicillin VK, Cephalexin, Dicloxacillin; Clindamycin or macrolide if penicillin allergic; MODERATE (systemic signs): Oral antibiotics adequate if reliable follow-up; Can add MSSA coverage (cephalexin, dicloxacillin); Consider IV if poor response anticipated (DM, immunocompromised); MRSA COVERAGE indicated if: Penetrating trauma/IVDU, Known MRSA colonization/infection, Failed beta-lactam therapy, SIRS present; Duration: 5 days typically (may extend if slow response)

questions.explanation: Non-Purulent Cellulitis Management: KEY POINT: Beta-hemolytic streptococci are primary cause. S. pyogenes is ALWAYS sensitive to penicillin! MILD (No Systemic Signs): Oral antibiotics targeting streptococci. First-line options: Penicillin VK 500mg QID, Cephalexin 500mg QID, Dicloxacillin 500mg QID. Penicillin allergy: Clindamycin 300-450mg TID, Azithromycin. MODERATE (Systemic Signs Present): Oral antibiotics still often adequate if patient can follow up. May include MSSA coverage: Cephalexin or dicloxacillin. Consider hospitalization for IV therapy if: Poor anticipated response (DM, immunocompromised), Facial cellulitis, Poor compliance/follow-up. WHEN TO ADD MRSA COVERAGE: Penetrating trauma (especially IVDU), Evidence of MRSA infection elsewhere or known colonization, Failed beta-lactam therapy, SIRS criteria met, Purulent drainage present. DURATION: 5 days typical. Extend to 7-10 days if slow response. ADJUNCTIVE: Leg elevation, Treat underlying tinea pedis (portal of entry). This patient: Moderate severity → oral cephalexin reasonable with close follow-up.

sources.text: StatPearls: Cellulitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient presents with a fluctuant 3cm abscess.
questions.question: What is the treatment approach for purulent skin infections?

options.text: I&D is optional for all abscesses
Beta-lactams are first-line for purulent infections

PURULENT SSTI (Abscess) TREATMENT: PRIMARY INTERVENTION = INCISION & DRAINAGE (I&D); MILD abscess (<2cm, no systemic signs): I&D ALONE may be sufficient - antibiotics optional (evidence shows reduced recurrence with abx but more side effects); MODERATE abscess (systemic signs): I&D + Empiric antibiotics covering MRSA; Options: TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs BID, Doxycycline 100mg BID, Clindamycin 300-450mg TID; SEVERE: I&D + IV antibiotics (vancomycin, daptomycin, linezolid); ALWAYS culture purulent material when possible; Duration: 5-10 days based on response
Antibiotics alone without I&D

questions.explanation: Purulent SSTI (Abscess) Management: KEY PRINCIPLE: INCISION AND DRAINAGE IS PRIMARY TREATMENT! Antibiotics without I&D = Treatment failure. MILD ABSCESS (Small, no systemic signs): I&D alone may be sufficient. Antibiotics OPTIONAL - meta-analyses show: Reduced recurrence with antibiotics, But increased adverse events, Shared decision-making appropriate. MODERATE PURULENT INFECTION (Systemic signs): I&D is essential. PLUS Empiric antibiotics covering CA-MRSA: TMP-SMX DS (1-2 tabs BID) - most commonly used, Doxycycline 100mg BID, Clindamycin 300-450mg TID (check local resistance). Note: TMP-SMX and doxycycline have poor strep coverage - if surrounding cellulitis extensive, add beta-lactam. SEVERE PURULENT INFECTION: I&D + IV antibiotics: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (target trough 15-20), Daptomycin 4 mg/kg IV daily, Linezolid 600mg IV/PO BID. CULTURES: Always culture purulent material when treating with antibiotics. Guides de-escalation. Duration: 5-10 days based on clinical response.

sources.text: StatPearls: Cellulitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You're deciding whether to cover MRSA.
questions.question: When is MRSA coverage indicated for cellulitis?

options.text: MRSA coverage never needed for skin infections

MRSA COVERAGE INDICATIONS: PURULENT infection (abscess, draining cellulitis) - always cover MRSA empirically; NON-PURULENT cellulitis - cover MRSA if: Penetrating trauma (especially IVDU), Known MRSA colonization or prior MRSA infection, SIRS criteria met (severe infection), Failed beta-lactam therapy, Immunocompromised; ROUTINE MRSA coverage NOT needed for typical non-purulent cellulitis (96% respond to beta-lactams alone); ORAL options for MRSA: TMP-SMX, Doxycycline, Clindamycin; For dual Strep + MRSA coverage: Clindamycin alone OR TMP-SMX/Doxy + beta-lactam
All cellulitis requires MRSA coverage
Vancomycin is required for all outpatient MRSA

questions.explanation: When to Cover MRSA: PURULENT INFECTIONS: ALWAYS cover MRSA empirically. CA-MRSA is dominant pathogen (up to 59% of purulent SSTIs). NON-PURULENT CELLULITIS: Beta-lactams successful in 96% of patients. MRSA coverage indicated ONLY if: Penetrating trauma, Injection drug use (IVDU), Evidence of MRSA infection elsewhere, Known MRSA nasal colonization, SIRS criteria met, Failed initial beta-lactam therapy, Immunocompromised host. ORAL MRSA-ACTIVE AGENTS: TMP-SMX DS: 1-2 tabs BID (poor strep coverage - add beta-lactam if needed), Doxycycline 100mg BID (poor strep coverage - add beta-lactam if needed), Clindamycin 300-450mg TID (covers MRSA + strep, but check local resistance ~10-20%). FOR BOTH STREP AND MRSA: Clindamycin alone (if local resistance low), OR Combination: TMP-SMX (or doxycycline) + Amoxicillin (or cephalexin). IV OPTIONS FOR MRSA: Vancomycin, Daptomycin, Linezolid, Ceftaroline.

questions.stem: You're deciding disposition for this patient.
questions.question: When should patients with cellulitis be hospitalized?

options.text: HOSPITALIZATION INDICATIONS: SIRS criteria or hemodynamic instability; Concern for necrotizing fasciitis or deep space infection; Failed outpatient oral antibiotic therapy; Immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy, poorly controlled DM); Facial cellulitis (risk of cavernous sinus thrombosis); Poor adherence anticipated; Unable to tolerate oral intake; Significant comorbidities affecting healing; IV ANTIBIOTIC OPTIONS: Non-purulent: Cefazolin 2g IV q8h (add vancomycin if MRSA risk); Purulent/MRSA: Vancomycin IV; Severe/Immunocompromised: Vancomycin + Piperacillin-tazobactam
Diabetes alone requires hospitalization
All cellulitis requires hospitalization
Outpatient treatment always appropriate if no abscess

questions.explanation: Hospitalization Criteria: INDICATIONS FOR ADMISSION: Hemodynamic instability or sepsis/SIRS, Concern for necrotizing soft tissue infection (NSTI), Concern for deep space infection (septic joint, osteomyelitis), Failed outpatient oral antibiotic therapy, Immunocompromised host (HIV/AIDS, transplant, chemotherapy, neutropenia), Poorly controlled diabetes with severe infection, Facial cellulitis (risk of intracranial extension), Poor anticipated adherence, Unable to tolerate oral antibiotics, Significant comorbidities. IV ANTIBIOTIC SELECTION: NON-PURULENT (No MRSA risk): Cefazolin 2g IV q8h (or ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily), Penicillin allergy: Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h. NON-PURULENT with MRSA risk: Add vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h. PURULENT/MRSA: Vancomycin (target trough 15-20 µg/mL), Alternatives: Daptomycin 4 mg/kg IV daily, Linezolid 600mg IV q12h. SEVERE/IMMUNOCOMPROMISED: Vancomycin + Piperacillin-tazobactam (broad coverage). This patient: Moderate severity, can likely be managed as outpatient with close follow-up given DM.

sources.text: StatPearls: Cellulitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to recognize when cellulitis may be something worse.
questions.question: What are the warning signs of necrotizing soft tissue infection?

options.text: RED FLAGS FOR NECROTIZING FASCIITIS: Pain out of proportion to exam findings; Rapid progression despite antibiotics; Crepitus on palpation (gas in tissues); Skin changes: Bullae, Dusky/purple discoloration, Skin necrosis/sloughing, Ecchymosis; Systemic toxicity (sepsis, multi-organ dysfunction); Wooden-hard induration; Anesthesia over affected area (nerve destruction); LRINEC SCORE: WBC, Hgb, Na, Glucose, Creatinine, CRP - helps risk stratify; MANAGEMENT: Immediate surgical exploration and debridement; Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Vanc + Pip-Tazo + Clindamycin); Do NOT delay surgery for imaging
MRI must be done before surgery
Antibiotics alone are adequate treatment
Pain level correlates with visual appearance

questions.explanation: Necrotizing Soft Tissue Infection (NSTI) Red Flags: CLINICAL WARNING SIGNS: Pain out of proportion to exam (classic - severe pain with minimal findings), Rapid spread despite appropriate antibiotics, Crepitus (gas in soft tissues - suggests Clostridial or mixed infection), Bullae/blisters, Dusky, purplish, or mottled skin, Skin necrosis or sloughing, Ecchymosis without trauma, Woody/hard induration (beyond area of erythema), Cutaneous anesthesia (nerve destruction), Systemic toxicity out of proportion to local findings. LRINEC SCORE (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis): CRP ≥150 mg/L (4 points), WBC 15-25K (1 pt) or >25K (2 pts), Hemoglobin <13.5 (1 pt) or <11 (2 pts), Sodium <135 (2 pts), Creatinine >1.6 (2 pts), Glucose >180 (1 pt). Score ≥6: High suspicion for NSTI. MANAGEMENT: SURGICAL EMERGENCY - immediate debridement. Do NOT delay surgery for imaging. Broad-spectrum antibiotics: Vancomycin + Piperacillin-tazobactam + Clindamycin (toxin suppression). ICU admission for resuscitation. Serial debridements often needed.

questions.stem: This 42-year-old diabetic has non-purulent moderate cellulitis.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: TMP-SMX alone for all cellulitis
IV vancomycin required

Complete approach: (1) CLASSIFICATION: Non-purulent cellulitis (no drainage/abscess) + Moderate severity (fever, but not SIRS); (2) MRSA RISK: Low - no IVDU, no prior MRSA, no penetrating trauma → Standard strep coverage adequate initially; (3) ANTIBIOTIC SELECTION: Cephalexin 500mg PO QID × 5-7 days (covers strep + MSSA given DM); (4) ADJUNCTIVE: Leg elevation, Treat tinea pedis (portal of entry - topical antifungal), Optimize glycemic control; (5) MARK BORDERS with pen to monitor progression; (6) CLOSE FOLLOW-UP: 48-72 hours - if not improving, consider IV therapy or MRSA coverage; (7) RETURN PRECAUTIONS: Worsening pain, spreading redness, bullae, skin color change
No antibiotics needed

questions.explanation: Complete Management for this patient: STEP 1 - CLASSIFY INFECTION: Non-purulent: No abscess, no drainage, no fluctuance. Moderate severity: Fever 100.6°F present, but not meeting full SIRS criteria. STEP 2 - ASSESS MRSA RISK: NO high-risk features: No IVDU, No known MRSA colonization/infection, No penetrating trauma, No failed beta-lactam therapy. → MRSA coverage NOT initially required. STEP 3 - ANTIBIOTIC SELECTION: Target: Beta-hemolytic streptococci (± MSSA given diabetes). Choice: Cephalexin 500mg PO QID (covers strep and MSSA). Alternative: Dicloxacillin 500mg PO QID. Duration: 5 days initially, extend to 7-10 if slow response. STEP 4 - ADJUNCTIVE MEASURES: Leg elevation (reduces edema, improves penetration). Treat tinea pedis (portal of entry): Topical antifungal (terbinafine, clotrimazole). Optimize glycemic control (improves healing). STEP 5 - MONITORING: Mark erythema borders with pen. Follow-up in 48-72 hours. If not improving: Consider hospitalization for IV antibiotics, Add MRSA coverage. STEP 6 - RETURN PRECAUTIONS: Worsening redness/spreading beyond marks, Increasing pain (especially out of proportion), Bullae, skin darkening, or necrosis, High fevers, altered mental status.

sources.text: StatPearls: Cellulitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected cellulitis abscess?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in cellulitis abscess. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of cellulitis abscess. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing cellulitis abscess, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
